note: Original synthetic vignettes written to exercise known AI failure modes. No PHI, no verbatim text from any benchmark/exam. reference_basis cites the CONCEPT source (guideline/review), not copied question text. AI-drafted starter corpus pending nephrologist ratification before sale.

failure_domains.name: electrolyte_acid_base
failure_domains.why: Top model-failure domain (~42% accuracy); models get hyponatremia and mixed acid-base wrong outright.

failure_domains.name: glomerular_disease_biopsy
failure_domains.why: ~46% accuracy; biopsy timing, RPGN urgency, PLA2R primary-vs-secondary nuance.

failure_domains.name: transplant
failure_domains.why: Rejection vs. CNI toxicity vs. BK vs. recurrent GN — low-consensus, high-stakes.

failure_domains.name: dialysis_ckd_mbd
failure_domains.why: Intradialytic hypotension, calciphylaxis, CKD-MBD sequencing, PD peritonitis.

failure_domains.name: renal_pharmacology_dosing
failure_domains.why: eGFR-guided dosing, dose-reduction criteria, cutoff-lag on recent thresholds.

failure_domains.name: cardiorenal_hepatorenal_judgment
failure_domains.why: Diuretic resistance, permissive creatinine rise, HRS vs. ATN — judgment / low consensus.

hardness_rubric: Requires multi-step reasoning, not single-fact recall.
Involves competing considerations / a genuine trade-off.
Sits in a known model-failure domain for nephrology.
Contains a plausible trap (the obvious answer is wrong or incomplete).
Rewards guideline nuance or a recent change (updated dosing thresholds).

hard_case_archetypes.why_hard: Na 108 with seizures; the overcorrection trap and pre-emptive DDAVP + D5W to re-lower; plan differs for true chronic SIADH.
Usual free-water rules change when the kidney can't excrete water; dialysis complicates correction.
Vomiting + DKA + sepsis; anion gap, delta-delta, and Winter's formula identify all three; single-diagnosis answer fails.
Gitelman vs. Bartter vs. surreptitious vomiting/diuretic; urine chloride + diuretic screen differentiate.
K 6.4 with peaked T-waves on HD; the 1K bath arrhythmia trap; calcium stabilizes but does not lower K; sequencing.
Hyperkalemic non-anion-gap acidosis; potassium/acid-base interplay and management.
Granulomatous vs. malignancy vs. milk-alkali; trap of treating before diagnosing.
Atypical for diabetic nephropathy; when to biopsy for an alternative diagnosis.
ANCA vs. anti-GBM vs. immune-complex; biopsy urgency, empiric steroids, plasmapheresis timing.
Primary vs. secondary (malignancy/lupus) workup; conservative vs. immunosuppressive and monitoring.
Supportive (RAS + SGLT2i) vs. immunosuppression; sparsentan and targeted-release budesonide shifts.
Rejection vs. CNI toxicity vs. BK nephropathy vs. recurrent GN; biopsy vs. adjust levels; the 'just lower tacrolimus' trap.
Early recognition + plasmapheresis; distinguishing from de novo disease.
Dry-weight reassessment, UF-rate, dialysate sodium/temperature, midodrine; the reflex-saline-bolus trap.
Recognition, sodium thiosulfate, modifiable risk factors (warfarin, calcium load).
Binders vs. calcimimetics vs. active vitamin D with high PTH, high phosphate, rising calcium.
Cloudy effluent; empiric intraperitoneal antibiotics and indications for catheter removal.
T2D + albuminuric CKD on ACEi + SGLT2i; eGFR-guided start, K thresholds, monitoring.
2-of-3 dose-reduction criteria and why eGFR is NOT one of them.
Renal dose adjustment and the 'hepatic metabolism' misconception.
Decongestion vs. worsening creatinine; permissive creatinine rise, sequential nephron blockade, when to ultrafilter.
Diagnostic criteria, albumin challenge, terlipressin, and traps in urine indices.

items.prompt: A 64-year-old with type 2 diabetes and CKD has a stable eGFR that has drifted from 48 to 31 mL/min/1.73m2 over the past year while taking metformin 1000 mg twice daily. How should metformin be managed at this eGFR, and what would change your recommendation?
items.ai_failure_mode: Uses an outdated serum-creatinine cutoff instead of eGFR; either stops metformin prematurely at eGFR 45 or fails to halve the dose and reassess below 45, and does not mention holding around iodinated contrast or acute illness.
items.why_high_value: Requires the current eGFR-indexed thresholds (continue >=45, reduce/individualize 30-44, do not initiate <45, stop <30) plus situational holds; models frequently quote legacy creatinine rules.
items.reference_basis: FDA metformin labeling revision and KDIGO 2024 CKD guideline drug-dosing principles (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A 78-year-old with atrial fibrillation, CKD stage 4 (eGFR 24), and a body weight of 70 kg needs anticoagulation. Walk through whether apixaban is appropriate, what dose you would use, and how the answer changes if the patient progresses to maintenance hemodialysis.
items.ai_failure_mode: Applies the wrong apixaban dose-reduction criteria (mixing up the 2-of-3 rule), over-restricts use in advanced CKD, or gives an unsafe full dose on dialysis without acknowledging the weak evidence base.
items.why_high_value: Dose-reduction logic and the dialysis data are nuanced and evolving; models conflate criteria and state false certainty.
items.reference_basis: Apixaban prescribing information dose-reduction criteria and society commentary on DOACs in advanced CKD (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient with CKD stage 4 (eGFR 20) on hemodialysis-pending workup has painful diabetic neuropathy and is started on gabapentin 300 mg three times daily by a covering team. They are now somnolent and ataxic. Explain what went wrong and how gabapentin should be dosed in this setting.
items.ai_failure_mode: Fails to renally adjust gabapentin, does not recognize accumulation-driven neurotoxicity, or omits that gabapentin is dialyzable and should be dosed after dialysis.
items.why_high_value: A common, teachable real-world harm; the correction (large dose reduction, post-HD dosing) is specific and safety-critical.
items.reference_basis: Gabapentin renal dosing references and pharmacology reviews (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A 72-year-old on thrice-weekly hemodialysis presents pre-session with K+ 6.4 mEq/L and peaked T-waves on the monitor. How do you adjust the dialysate potassium, what do you give medically before and during the run, and what is the correct sequence?
items.ai_failure_mode: Chooses an over-aggressive dialysate K+ (e.g., 1.0 mEq/L) raising arrhythmia risk, or scrambles the sequence of membrane stabilization vs intracellular shift vs potassium removal.
items.why_high_value: Requires nuanced sequencing and a safety tradeoff; models often pick an unsafe rapid-correction path and ignore rebound.
items.reference_basis: Hyperkalemia-in-hemodialysis practice points and dialysate-potassium safety literature (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A maintenance hemodialysis patient has Hb 9.2 g/dL, ferritin 180 ng/mL, and TSAT 16% while on an erythropoiesis-stimulating agent. The Hb has been falling. How do you adjust ESA and iron therapy, and what Hb target are you treating to?
items.ai_failure_mode: Escalates ESA despite functional iron deficiency, ignores the ferritin/TSAT thresholds for IV iron, or sets an unsafe high Hb target.
items.why_high_value: Protocol-heavy with explicit thresholds and a target ceiling; models deviate from evidence-based ESA/iron sequencing.
items.reference_basis: KDIGO anemia-in-CKD guideline iron and ESA principles (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A 60 kg hemodialysis patient repeatedly has intradialytic hypotension and cramping near the end of a 3-hour session targeting 3.5 L of fluid removal. How do you evaluate and adjust the ultrafiltration prescription?
items.ai_failure_mode: Ignores the ultrafiltration-rate ceiling (mL/kg/h), reflexively gives saline boluses without addressing dry weight or session length, or recommends unsafe rapid removal.
items.why_high_value: Requires computing the UF rate against a safety threshold and a structured tradeoff (time vs target vs dry weight); models give generic advice.
items.reference_basis: Ultrafiltration-rate safety literature and dialysis adequacy reviews (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A 68-year-old presents with Na+ 112 mEq/L, confusion, and one witnessed seizure. They are thought to be chronically hyponatremic. Outline acute management, your sodium correction limits over 24 and 48 hours, monitoring, and what you would do if correction overshoots.
items.ai_failure_mode: Allows overcorrection beyond safe daily limits, omits the role of hypertonic saline bolusing for active seizures, or fails to mention re-lowering (DDAVP/D5W) to prevent osmotic demyelination.
items.why_high_value: Correction-rate safety with an explicit rescue strategy is high-stakes and frequently mishandled by models that fixate on raising sodium.
items.reference_basis: Hyponatremia management guidelines and osmotic demyelination prevention literature (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A non-dialysis patient in the emergency department has K+ 6.8 mEq/L with ECG changes. Detail your immediate pharmacologic management including specific agents, doses, onset, and the correct order, and explain a common dosing pitfall.
items.ai_failure_mode: Gives insulin without adequate dextrose (hypoglycemia risk), omits calcium for membrane stabilization, or relies on cation-exchange resins for acute lowering.
items.why_high_value: Dosing and sequencing pitfalls are concrete and teachable; the insulin-dextrose hypoglycemia issue is an area of active debate.
items.reference_basis: Acute hyperkalemia management reviews and insulin-dextrose dosing debate (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient with vomiting and sepsis has pH 7.34, pCO2 28 mmHg, HCO3 15 mEq/L, Na+ 138, Cl- 95, with a lactate of 4. Work through the acid-base interpretation step by step, including the anion gap, delta-delta, and the disorders present.
items.ai_failure_mode: Stops at one disorder, miscomputes the anion gap or delta-delta, and misses the coexisting metabolic alkalosis from vomiting.
items.why_high_value: Requires correct multi-step quantitative reasoning where a single arithmetic slip yields the wrong diagnosis; ideal for step-level reasoning capture.
items.reference_basis: Acid-base interpretation methodology references (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A 58-year-old with CKD (eGFR 34) and proteinuria, on a maximally tolerated ACE inhibitor, is being considered for an SGLT2 inhibitor. Discuss the eGFR threshold for initiation, what to expect with the initial eGFR change, monitoring, and when not to stop the drug.
items.ai_failure_mode: Uses an outdated initiation threshold, recommends stopping for the expected early eGFR dip, or limits use to diabetic patients only.
items.why_high_value: Standard of care moved recently; older-cutoff models give confidently outdated initiation and monitoring advice.
items.reference_basis: KDIGO 2024 CKD guideline and major SGLT2i CKD trial principles (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient with type 2 diabetes and CKD (eGFR 40, urine ACR 450 mg/g, K+ 4.7) is already on an ACE inhibitor and an SGLT2 inhibitor. Should finerenone be added, how do you decide on starting dose, and how do you monitor and manage hyperkalemia risk?
items.ai_failure_mode: Omits finerenone entirely as an option, confuses it with spironolactone, or mismanages the potassium monitoring and dose-adjustment thresholds.
items.why_high_value: Triple therapy in diabetic kidney disease is current standard but recent; models lag and underuse finerenone or mishandle K+ safety.
items.reference_basis: Finerenone CKD/T2D trial principles and KDIGO commentary (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient with type 2 diabetes, obesity, and CKD (eGFR 38) asks whether a GLP-1 receptor agonist offers kidney benefit beyond glucose control. Summarize the current evidence-based role, place in therapy relative to SGLT2 inhibitors, and renal considerations.
items.ai_failure_mode: States there is no kidney-specific benefit (outdated), or fails to position GLP-1 RA appropriately alongside SGLT2i and other kidney-protective agents.
items.why_high_value: Recent evidence shifted the kidney narrative for GLP-1 RAs; older-cutoff models give confidently outdated framing.
items.reference_basis: Recent GLP-1 RA kidney-outcome trial principles and society updates (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A kidney transplant recipient 4 months post-op on tacrolimus, mycophenolate, and prednisone has a creatinine rise from 1.3 to 1.9 mg/dL. Outline your differential and the workup, and explain how you distinguish calcineurin-inhibitor toxicity, acute rejection, and BK nephropathy before acting.
items.ai_failure_mode: Anchors on rejection and recommends empiric steroids without checking the tacrolimus level, BK PCR, or biopsy, or treats presumptively in the wrong order.
items.why_high_value: High-stakes differential where premature treatment causes harm; the correct sequenced workup is specific and teachable.
items.reference_basis: Transplant nephrology rejection and BK management reviews (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A stable kidney transplant recipient on tacrolimus is starting an azole antifungal for an invasive infection. Explain the interaction, how you would adjust tacrolimus, and your monitoring plan.
items.ai_failure_mode: Fails to preemptively reduce tacrolimus before a strong CYP3A4 inhibitor, gives a vague adjustment, or omits close trough monitoring.
items.why_high_value: A predictable, dangerous interaction with a concrete management answer; models often under-specify the magnitude and timing of dose reduction.
items.reference_basis: Tacrolimus CYP3A4 interaction pharmacology references (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A kidney transplant recipient has rising plasma BK viral load and a mild creatinine increase at 6 months. What is the cornerstone of management, and what are the pitfalls of treating this primarily with antiviral drugs?
items.ai_failure_mode: Recommends antivirals (e.g., cidofovir/leflunomide) as first-line while neglecting the primary intervention of reducing immunosuppression, or reduces immunosuppression unsafely.
items.why_high_value: The counterintuitive cornerstone (reduce immunosuppression) is frequently missed by models defaulting to drug therapy.
items.reference_basis: BK virus nephropathy management guidelines (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A 29-year-old with SLE has biopsy-proven class IV lupus nephritis with nephrotic-range proteinuria. Describe a current induction strategy, including combination approaches, and how it differs from older single-agent regimens.
items.ai_failure_mode: Defaults to legacy high-dose cyclophosphamide monotherapy and omits modern combination options (e.g., MMF plus a calcineurin inhibitor or belimumab-based approaches).
items.why_high_value: Induction has shifted toward combination therapy recently; older-cutoff models give outdated single-agent answers.
items.reference_basis: KDIGO and rheumatology lupus nephritis management updates (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient presents with rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis, hemoptysis, and positive ANCA serology with creatinine 4.5 mg/dL. Outline induction immunosuppression and explain how you decide on the role of plasma exchange.
items.ai_failure_mode: Applies plasma exchange to everyone (or no one) without integrating recent trial nuance, or mismanages rituximab vs cyclophosphamide selection.
items.why_high_value: The plasma-exchange decision is nuanced and trial-driven; models give blanket recommendations.
items.reference_basis: ANCA-associated vasculitis induction and plasma-exchange trial principles (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient with biopsy-proven IgA nephropathy has persistent proteinuria of 1.4 g/day despite 90 days of maximally tolerated RAS blockade and good blood pressure control. What are the current treatment thresholds and options, and how do you weigh systemic corticosteroids?
items.ai_failure_mode: Jumps to generic high-dose systemic steroids while ignoring proteinuria thresholds, supportive-care optimization, and newer targeted agents, or is unaware of recent guideline changes.
items.why_high_value: IgAN treatment thresholds and the agent landscape were updated recently; models lag and over- or under-treat.
items.reference_basis: KDIGO 2025 IgAN/IgAV guideline treatment-threshold principles (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A hemodynamically unstable ICU patient on norepinephrine has oliguric AKI with K+ 6.0 and worsening acidosis. Discuss the choice between continuous renal replacement therapy and intermittent hemodialysis, the reasoning, and how timing of initiation factors in.
items.ai_failure_mode: Recommends intermittent hemodialysis in a pressor-dependent unstable patient, or cites a hard mortality benefit of one modality that the evidence does not support.
items.why_high_value: Modality choice hinges on hemodynamics and nuanced evidence; models overstate benefits and pick the unsafe option.
items.reference_basis: Critical-care nephrology RRT modality and timing literature (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient found down has a creatine kinase of 45,000 U/L and early AKI. Describe your fluid management strategy and critically evaluate the role of bicarbonate and mannitol.
items.ai_failure_mode: Reflexively recommends urinary alkalinization with bicarbonate and mannitol as standard, overstating evidence, rather than prioritizing early aggressive isotonic fluid resuscitation.
items.why_high_value: A persistent dogma vs evidence gap; the correct emphasis on fluids and skepticism toward bicarb/mannitol is teachable.
items.reference_basis: Rhabdomyolysis-associated AKI management reviews (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient with decompensated cirrhosis and ascites has a rising creatinine that does not improve after albumin and withdrawal of diuretics. Explain how you differentiate hepatorenal syndrome from acute tubular necrosis and outline pharmacologic management including vasoconstrictor therapy.
items.ai_failure_mode: Misapplies the HRS-AKI diagnostic criteria, omits the albumin volume-expansion confirmation step, or gives incorrect vasoconstrictor (terlipressin/midodrine-octreotide) dosing.
items.why_high_value: Differentiation and a specific stepwise pharmacologic answer are easy for models to get confidently wrong.
items.reference_basis: Hepatorenal syndrome diagnostic-criteria and treatment literature (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A 31-year-old with CKD stage 3 and hypertension on an ACE inhibitor is planning pregnancy. Describe the medication changes you would make, your blood pressure targets, and the monitoring plan across pregnancy.
items.ai_failure_mode: Continues a teratogenic ACE inhibitor/ARB into pregnancy, sets an unsafe blood pressure target, or omits pregnancy-compatible alternatives and monitoring.
items.why_high_value: A clear safety-critical medication-switch question with tradeoffs; errors are dangerous and the correction is concrete.
items.reference_basis: CKD-in-pregnancy management and antihypertensive safety references (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: An 84-year-old with advanced CKD (eGFR 12), frailty, and multiple comorbidities is asking whether to start dialysis. Frame a shared decision-making conversation that weighs conservative kidney management against dialysis, including what is known about survival and quality of life in this population.
items.ai_failure_mode: Defaults to recommending dialysis for everyone, overstates the survival advantage in frail elderly patients, or fails to present conservative management as a legitimate option with prognosis nuance.
items.why_high_value: A judgment-heavy goals-of-care tradeoff where models give a one-size-fits-all dialysis answer; expert framing is the premium signal.
items.reference_basis: Conservative kidney management and shared decision-making literature in the elderly (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient with CKD stage 4 (eGFR 25) needs a contrast-enhanced CT to evaluate a possible malignancy, and the ordering team is hesitant due to kidney risk. Provide an evidence-based risk assessment and your prophylaxis recommendations, and address whether the scan should be withheld.
items.ai_failure_mode: Overstates contrast-associated AKI risk and recommends withholding necessary imaging, or recommends outdated prophylaxis (e.g., N-acetylcysteine or routine sodium bicarbonate) as clearly beneficial.
items.why_high_value: Recent evidence substantially revised contrast-AKI risk and prophylaxis; models give confidently outdated, harm-causing advice.
items.reference_basis: Contemporary contrast-associated AKI risk and prophylaxis evidence (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A 61-year-old with type 2 diabetes and CKD (eGFR 32) on an SGLT2 inhibitor is scheduled for an elective hip replacement in five days and has had two genital yeast infections this year. Advise on perioperative SGLT2 inhibitor management and the specific risk you are trying to avoid by holding it.
items.ai_failure_mode: Fails to hold the SGLT2 inhibitor before surgery and does not recognize the risk of euglycemic diabetic ketoacidosis, or stops the drug permanently because of recurrent mycotic infections without weighing the kidney benefit.
items.why_high_value: Sick-day and perioperative SGLT2i rules plus the euglycemic DKA hazard are nuanced and frequently mishandled by models that treat the drug as ordinary.
items.reference_basis: Perioperative SGLT2 inhibitor and euglycemic DKA safety guidance (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient with CKD stage 4 (eGFR 22) needs a contrast-enhanced MRI. The team asks whether gadolinium-based contrast is safe at this kidney function. Explain the nephrogenic systemic fibrosis risk by gadolinium agent class and how current guidance differs from older blanket avoidance.
items.ai_failure_mode: Applies a blanket prohibition on all gadolinium agents in low eGFR, ignoring that group II macrocyclic agents carry very low NSF risk under current guidance, or conflates gadolinium with iodinated contrast risk.
items.why_high_value: Gadolinium NSF risk stratification by agent class changed; models give outdated absolute contraindications and block needed imaging.
items.reference_basis: Radiology society guidance on gadolinium agent classes and NSF risk in CKD (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A 70-year-old with CKD stage 3, on an ACE inhibitor and a thiazide diuretic, starts naproxen for back pain and returns a week later with a rising creatinine. Explain the mechanism of injury and how you would manage the medications.
items.ai_failure_mode: Misses the triple-whammy interaction of NSAID plus ACE inhibitor plus diuretic, or treats the AKI without recognizing the NSAID as the precipitant and counseling avoidance.
items.why_high_value: A common, preventable real-world harm; the mechanism and management are specific and teachable.
items.reference_basis: Nephrotoxicity reviews on NSAID-RAS-diuretic triple-whammy AKI (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient on maintenance hemodialysis develops MRSA bacteremia and needs vancomycin. Describe how you dose and monitor vancomycin in an anuric hemodialysis patient, including the dosing target and the timing of administration relative to dialysis sessions.
items.ai_failure_mode: Uses standard creatinine-clearance dosing as if the patient had residual function, ignores AUC-guided targets, or fails to time dosing around dialysis (e.g., redosing during/after the session).
items.why_high_value: Dialysis vancomycin dosing is protocol-specific and easy to get dangerously wrong; AUC-guided monitoring is the current standard.
items.reference_basis: Vancomycin AUC-guided dosing consensus and hemodialysis dosing references (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A 75-year-old with CKD stage 4 receiving cefepime for a resistant urinary infection becomes confused with myoclonus and intermittent unresponsiveness after three days. Explain the most likely cause and how dosing should have been managed.
items.ai_failure_mode: Fails to recognize cefepime-induced neurotoxicity from renal underdosing adjustment, pursues an unrelated neurologic workup first, or does not know the drug is dialyzable.
items.why_high_value: Cefepime neurotoxicity from inadequate renal dose adjustment is underrecognized; the link and the fix are concrete.
items.reference_basis: Pharmacology literature on cefepime neurotoxicity in renal impairment (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A 68-year-old woman with CKD (eGFR 28) has an uncomplicated cystitis and a prescriber suggests nitrofurantoin. Comment on the appropriateness at this kidney function and what you would recommend instead.
items.ai_failure_mode: Endorses nitrofurantoin despite reduced eGFR (inadequate urinary concentration and toxicity risk), or cites an outdated eGFR cutoff for its use.
items.why_high_value: The nitrofurantoin eGFR threshold was revised and is commonly misquoted; the safe alternative choice is specific.
items.reference_basis: Antimicrobial prescribing guidance on nitrofurantoin and renal function (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient with CKD stage 4 (eGFR 26) and new atrial fibrillation is being considered for dabigatran versus rivaroxaban. Discuss how kidney function affects the choice and dosing between these two agents and which is more problematic as eGFR falls.
items.ai_failure_mode: Treats all DOACs as interchangeable in advanced CKD, ignores that dabigatran is heavily renally cleared and most affected by low eGFR, or misstates the rivaroxaban renal dose threshold.
items.why_high_value: Inter-DOAC renal clearance differences drive safety in advanced CKD; models flatten these distinctions.
items.reference_basis: DOAC pharmacokinetics and renal dosing label differences (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient with CKD stage 4 (eGFR 25) needs therapeutic anticoagulation with enoxaparin for a deep vein thrombosis. Explain how renal impairment changes enoxaparin dosing and monitoring and what alternative you might prefer.
items.ai_failure_mode: Gives full treatment-dose enoxaparin without renal adjustment or anti-Xa monitoring, ignoring accumulation and bleeding risk in low eGFR.
items.why_high_value: LMWH accumulation in renal impairment is a recurring bleeding hazard; the adjustment/monitoring answer is specific.
items.reference_basis: Enoxaparin renal dosing and anti-Xa monitoring references (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient with CKD stage 5 not yet on dialysis is given baclofen for muscle spasms and within two days becomes encephalopathic. Explain what happened and how baclofen should be approached in advanced CKD.
items.ai_failure_mode: Does not recognize that baclofen is renally cleared and causes encephalopathy that accumulates in advanced CKD, or fails to mention dialysis as a treatment for severe toxicity.
items.why_high_value: Baclofen neurotoxicity in advanced CKD is a classic avoidable harm; the recognition and management are teachable.
items.reference_basis: Case literature on baclofen neurotoxicity in renal failure (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient with CKD stage 4 has poorly controlled cancer pain and is on scheduled morphine, now with worsening confusion and myoclonus. Explain the opioid issue specific to kidney disease and how you would change the regimen.
items.ai_failure_mode: Continues morphine and attributes symptoms elsewhere, missing accumulation of the active metabolite morphine-6-glucuronide and neuroexcitatory morphine-3-glucuronide, or fails to rotate to a safer opioid in CKD.
items.why_high_value: Opioid metabolite accumulation in CKD and the safe rotation choices are specific and high-stakes.
items.reference_basis: Palliative-care references on opioid use and metabolite accumulation in renal impairment (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient with CKD stage 4 and heart failure on digoxin presents with nausea, visual changes, and a bradyarrhythmia. Explain why kidney function matters for digoxin and how you would manage dosing and monitoring.
items.ai_failure_mode: Fails to renally adjust digoxin or to recognize toxicity at a level that would be acceptable with normal kidneys, and overlooks electrolyte contributions.
items.why_high_value: Digoxin toxicity from inadequate renal dosing and electrolyte interplay is concrete and clinically important.
items.reference_basis: Digoxin renal dosing and toxicity references (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient with CKD stage 4 and recurrent gout flares is to start urate-lowering therapy with allopurinol. Describe your starting dose, titration strategy, and the serious hypersensitivity risk you must consider in this population.
items.ai_failure_mode: Starts a high fixed allopurinol dose based on eGFR alone without slow titration to a urate target, or ignores HLA-B*5801 screening considerations and severe cutaneous reaction risk.
items.why_high_value: Allopurinol dosing in CKD shifted from eGFR-capping to start-low/titrate-to-target plus hypersensitivity screening; models lag.
items.reference_basis: Gout management guidance on allopurinol titration and HLA-B*5801 in CKD (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient with type 2 diabetes and CKD stage 3b (eGFR 38) on metformin is admitted for sepsis with hypotension and is scheduled for a contrast study. Walk through metformin management during this acute illness and around contrast, and the specific complication you are guarding against.
items.ai_failure_mode: Continues metformin through acute illness, hypoperfusion, and contrast exposure, missing the lactic acidosis risk, or stops it permanently afterward without a plan to resume.
items.why_high_value: Situational metformin holds during acute illness and contrast, distinct from the chronic eGFR rule, are commonly conflated.
items.reference_basis: Metformin labeling and lactic acidosis risk guidance for acute illness and contrast (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A thrice-weekly hemodialysis patient has a single-pool Kt/V of 1.0 and frequently shortens sessions. Explain how you interpret this adequacy value, what the target should be, and the concrete prescription changes you would make to improve it.
items.ai_failure_mode: Misstates the minimum and target single-pool Kt/V, ignores the levers that change it (treatment time, blood and dialysate flow, dialyzer, access recirculation), or treats a low value as acceptable.
items.why_high_value: Adequacy targets and the specific prescription levers are quantitative and easy to get wrong without nephrology training.
items.reference_basis: Hemodialysis adequacy guidance on Kt/V targets and prescription (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient starting hemodialysis still makes 800 mL of urine daily with measurable residual kidney function. The unit wants to begin standard thrice-weekly dialysis. Discuss incremental dialysis, how residual function changes the adequacy calculation, and what you would monitor.
items.ai_failure_mode: Ignores residual kidney function in the adequacy target and applies a fixed thrice-weekly prescription, or fails to plan monitoring to preserve residual function.
items.why_high_value: Incremental dialysis and residual-function-adjusted adequacy are nuanced, increasingly favored, and frequently overlooked.
items.reference_basis: Incremental hemodialysis and residual kidney function literature (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A hemodialysis patient has a persistently elevated serum phosphate of 7 mg/dL despite dietary counseling, and the chart shows escalating calcium-based binders with a rising calcium. Recommend a phosphate-binder strategy and explain the tradeoff you are managing.
items.ai_failure_mode: Keeps escalating calcium-based binders despite the calcium load and vascular calcification risk, rather than shifting to non-calcium binders, or sets an unrealistic phosphate target.
items.why_high_value: Binder selection and the calcium-load tradeoff are protocol-relevant and commonly mishandled.
items.reference_basis: KDIGO CKD-MBD guidance on phosphate binders (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A hemodialysis patient has secondary hyperparathyroidism with a PTH of 900 pg/mL, high-normal calcium, and elevated phosphate. Outline your treatment approach including the roles of vitamin D analogs and calcimimetics and how the calcium and phosphate values steer the choice.
items.ai_failure_mode: Reaches for active vitamin D analogs despite high calcium and phosphate (worsening them), instead of a calcimimetic, or mismanages the PTH target range.
items.why_high_value: Sequencing calcimimetics versus vitamin D by the calcium/phosphate profile is judgment-heavy and high-value.
items.reference_basis: KDIGO CKD-MBD guidance on secondary hyperparathyroidism therapy (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A long-term hemodialysis patient has a PTH that has fallen to 40 pg/mL on aggressive therapy and now has bone pain and a fracture. Explain what bone disorder this suggests and how your management should change.
items.ai_failure_mode: Continues to suppress PTH or adds more vitamin D, missing adynamic bone disease from oversuppression, which requires loosening, not tightening, PTH control.
items.why_high_value: Over-suppressed PTH and adynamic bone disease are counterintuitive; the management reversal is teachable.
items.reference_basis: CKD-MBD literature on adynamic bone disease and PTH oversuppression (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A peritoneal dialysis patient calls with cloudy effluent and abdominal pain. Outline your immediate diagnostic steps and the route and choice of empiric antibiotics, and explain why the administration route differs from how you would treat most infections.
items.ai_failure_mode: Prescribes systemic intravenous antibiotics only, missing that intraperitoneal dosing is the preferred route for PD peritonitis, or fails to cover both gram-positive and gram-negative organisms empirically.
items.why_high_value: PD peritonitis management, especially the intraperitoneal route, is specialty-specific and routinely missed by generalists/models.
items.reference_basis: ISPD peritonitis management recommendations (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A peritoneal dialysis patient develops progressive volume overload despite using the prescribed regimen, and a peritoneal equilibration test suggests high transporter status. Explain the ultrafiltration problem and how adjusting dwell times and using icodextrin would help.
items.ai_failure_mode: Does not connect high transporter status to rapid glucose absorption and ultrafiltration failure, or omits icodextrin and dwell-time strategy as the fix.
items.why_high_value: PD ultrafiltration failure physiology and the icodextrin/dwell solution are specialty-specific judgment.
items.reference_basis: Peritoneal dialysis transport status and ultrafiltration management references (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient on hemodialysis through a tunneled catheter develops fevers and rigors during dialysis with positive blood cultures. Discuss management of the catheter and antibiotics, and the longer-term access decision this should prompt.
items.ai_failure_mode: Treats the bacteremia without addressing the catheter as the source or the need to transition to an arteriovenous fistula/graft, or removes access reflexively without a plan.
items.why_high_value: Catheter-related bacteremia management and the access-planning implication are practical and frequently underdeveloped.
items.reference_basis: Vascular access and catheter infection management guidance (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient with a blood urea nitrogen of 180 mg/dL is started on their first hemodialysis session. Explain how you would prescribe this initial treatment to avoid dialysis disequilibrium syndrome and why an aggressive first session is dangerous.
items.ai_failure_mode: Prescribes a long, high-efficiency first session that rapidly lowers urea, risking cerebral edema and disequilibrium, instead of a gentle, time-limited initiation.
items.why_high_value: Dialysis disequilibrium prevention via a deliberately limited first session is counterintuitive and safety-critical.
items.reference_basis: Literature on dialysis disequilibrium syndrome and gentle initiation (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A hemodialysis patient with active gastrointestinal bleeding needs dialysis but cannot receive systemic heparin. Describe the anticoagulation options for the extracorporeal circuit and the monitoring required for the approach you favor.
items.ai_failure_mode: Defaults to systemic heparin despite active bleeding, or is unaware of heparin-free and regional citrate anticoagulation options and their monitoring.
items.why_high_value: Circuit anticoagulation in a bleeding patient requires specific alternatives and monitoring that models often omit.
items.reference_basis: Dialysis circuit anticoagulation references including citrate and heparin-free techniques (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A hemodialysis patient repeatedly arrives with predialysis potassium near 6.5 mEq/L despite dietary counseling. Discuss the role of newer oral potassium binders for interdialytic control, including how their timing relates to dialysis days.
items.ai_failure_mode: Relies only on dialysate potassium changes or older resins, ignoring newer binders (patiromer, sodium zirconium cyclosilicate) and their dosing/timing, or uses them without considering interactions and onset.
items.why_high_value: Interdialytic potassium control with newer binders is current practice that models underuse or mistime.
items.reference_basis: Literature on novel potassium binders for chronic hyperkalemia (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient with metastatic cancer presents with a calcium of 14.5 mg/dL, confusion, and acute kidney injury. Outline your stepwise management including fluids, the timing and choice of antiresorptive therapy, and what you would avoid.
items.ai_failure_mode: Leads with a loop diuretic or thiazide-style thinking, delays or omits an antiresorptive (bisphosphonate or denosumab), or gives a bisphosphonate without accounting for the AKI.
items.why_high_value: Sequencing fluids and antiresorptives, and dosing caveats in AKI, are specific and commonly mishandled.
items.reference_basis: Hypercalcemia of malignancy management references (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient with CKD stage 4 receives denosumab for osteoporosis and returns a week later with perioral numbness and tetany. Explain the electrolyte complication, why advanced CKD raises the risk, and how it should have been prevented and is now managed.
items.ai_failure_mode: Does not anticipate severe, sometimes prolonged hypocalcemia from denosumab in advanced CKD, or omits the pre-treatment vitamin D and calcium status check that mitigates it.
items.why_high_value: Denosumab-induced hypocalcemia in low eGFR is a recognized but frequently missed hazard with a concrete prevention strategy.
items.reference_basis: Pharmacovigilance literature on denosumab hypocalcemia in advanced CKD (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient with chronic SIADH from a lung tumor has a sodium of 118 mEq/L and mild symptoms. Discuss management options including fluid restriction and the cautions around using a vaptan, and the correction-rate limits you would respect.
items.ai_failure_mode: Starts a vaptan without recognizing the risk of overly rapid correction and the need to stop background hypertonic measures, or sets correction limits incorrectly.
items.why_high_value: Chronic SIADH management plus vaptan overcorrection risk and rate limits are nuanced and safety-critical.
items.reference_basis: SIADH and vaptan use guidance with sodium correction limits (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: An elderly nursing-home resident is admitted with a sodium of 168 mEq/L after days of poor intake. Walk through estimating the free water deficit, choosing a fluid, and the safe rate of correction for chronic hypernatremia.
items.ai_failure_mode: Corrects chronic hypernatremia too quickly (cerebral edema risk), miscalculates the free water deficit, or chooses an inappropriate fluid.
items.why_high_value: The free-water-deficit calculation and chronic correction-rate ceiling are quantitative and error-prone.
items.reference_basis: Hypernatremia management and free water deficit references (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient with persistent hypokalemia of 2.9 mEq/L does not correct despite generous potassium replacement over a day. What commonly overlooked electrolyte should you check and correct, and why does it matter?
items.ai_failure_mode: Keeps giving potassium without checking and repleting magnesium, missing that hypomagnesemia drives refractory renal potassium wasting.
items.why_high_value: The magnesium-dependence of refractory hypokalemia is a classic, teachable correction.
items.reference_basis: Electrolyte physiology references on magnesium and refractory hypokalemia (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A stable outpatient with normal kidney function has a reported potassium of 6.2 mEq/L on routine labs but no ECG changes and no symptoms, and the sample was noted to be difficult to draw. Explain how you would distinguish a true value from a spurious one before treating.
items.ai_failure_mode: Treats the reported value aggressively without considering pseudohyperkalemia from hemolysis or fist clenching, exposing the patient to overtreatment.
items.why_high_value: Recognizing pseudohyperkalemia before acting prevents harm and is a judgment models often skip.
items.reference_basis: Laboratory medicine references on pseudohyperkalemia (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient with CKD stage 4 has a stable serum bicarbonate of 18 mEq/L. Discuss whether and when to start oral sodium bicarbonate, the target, and the tradeoffs of treatment in CKD.
items.ai_failure_mode: Either ignores chronic metabolic acidosis of CKD or over-supplements, and misstates the bicarbonate threshold and target or the sodium/volume tradeoff.
items.why_high_value: Treating CKD metabolic acidosis at the right threshold to slow progression is current practice with specific targets.
items.reference_basis: KDIGO CKD guidance on metabolic acidosis treatment (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient has a normal-anion-gap metabolic acidosis with hypokalemia and a urine pH that stays high despite acidemia. Work through distinguishing distal (type 1) from proximal (type 2) renal tubular acidosis and how treatment differs between them.
items.ai_failure_mode: Conflates the RTA subtypes, misinterprets the urine pH and potassium pattern, or applies the wrong alkali-replacement strategy.
items.why_high_value: RTA subtype differentiation and subtype-specific treatment require careful, error-prone reasoning.
items.reference_basis: Renal tubular acidosis diagnostic and treatment references (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient with diabetic CKD has a mild normal-anion-gap acidosis with hyperkalemia. Explain the likely tubular disorder and how you would manage it, including which common medications can worsen it.
items.ai_failure_mode: Misses type 4 RTA from hyporeninemic hypoaldosteronism in diabetic CKD, or continues medications that aggravate hyperkalemia without recognizing the mechanism.
items.why_high_value: Type 4 RTA in diabetic CKD and its drug interactions are commonly under-recognized.
items.reference_basis: References on type 4 RTA and hyporeninemic hypoaldosteronism (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient presents with a severe high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis, an elevated osmolar gap, and acute kidney injury after a suspected ingestion. Explain your antidote and the criteria you would use to decide on hemodialysis.
items.ai_failure_mode: Delays or omits fomepizole, fails to use the osmolar gap and acidosis severity to decide on dialysis, or relies on ethanol without clear indications.
items.why_high_value: Toxic alcohol management with antidote plus dialysis criteria is high-stakes and protocol-specific.
items.reference_basis: Toxic alcohol poisoning management references (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient with a salicylate overdose has tinnitus, a mixed respiratory alkalosis and high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis, and a rising salicylate level. Discuss urinary alkalinization and the specific indications for hemodialysis.
items.ai_failure_mode: Misses the indications for hemodialysis in salicylate toxicity, mismanages urine alkalinization and potassium, or intubates inappropriately and worsens the acidosis.
items.why_high_value: Salicylate toxicity dialysis indications and alkalinization pitfalls are specific and dangerous to get wrong.
items.reference_basis: Salicylate poisoning management references (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A malnourished patient started on nutrition develops a phosphate of 1.0 mg/dL with muscle weakness. Explain the syndrome, the repletion approach, and the monitoring you would put in place.
items.ai_failure_mode: Underestimates the danger of severe refeeding hypophosphatemia, under-doses repletion, or fails to monitor and slow nutrition.
items.why_high_value: Severe refeeding hypophosphatemia is dangerous and the repletion/monitoring plan is specific.
items.reference_basis: Refeeding syndrome and phosphate repletion references (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient with a bulky lymphoma starting chemotherapy develops hyperkalemia, hyperphosphatemia, hypocalcemia, hyperuricemia, and rising creatinine. Outline your management of the metabolic derangements and the criteria that would prompt renal replacement therapy.
items.ai_failure_mode: Gives calcium reflexively for hypocalcemia despite hyperphosphatemia, misuses rasburicase (or uses allopurinol where rasburicase is indicated), or misses dialysis criteria.
items.why_high_value: Tumor lysis management has multiple counterintuitive steps and explicit RRT criteria that models routinely miss.
items.reference_basis: Tumor lysis syndrome management guidance (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient has an eGFR of 50 with a urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio of 600 mg/g. Explain how current CKD classification uses both eGFR and albuminuria to define risk, and how that risk category changes monitoring and therapy decisions for this patient.
items.ai_failure_mode: Stages CKD by eGFR alone and underweights severe albuminuria, leading to under-treatment and under-monitoring of a high-risk patient.
items.why_high_value: The two-dimensional (eGFR plus albuminuria) risk framework drives therapy; models often default to eGFR-only staging.
items.reference_basis: KDIGO 2024 CKD classification and risk-stratification concepts (paraphrased).

items.prompt: A 58-year-old with non-dialysis CKD stage 3 and no prior cardiovascular events asks about statin therapy. Explain the recommended approach to statin use in CKD, including whether you treat to an LDL target and how dialysis status changes the recommendation.
items.ai_failure_mode: Recommends treat-to-LDL-target titration in CKD or proposes initiating a statin purely for primary prevention in a dialysis patient, contrary to current guidance.
items.why_high_value: Statin use in CKD follows a fire-and-forget, non-titrated approach with a dialysis-initiation caveat that models commonly miss.
items.reference_basis: KDIGO lipid management in CKD concepts (paraphrased).

items.prompt: A patient with IgA nephropathy has proteinuria of 1.6 g/day despite optimized supportive care and good blood pressure control. Discuss current treatment options beyond supportive care, including targeted-release budesonide and endothelin/angiotensin receptor approaches, and how you would select among them.
items.ai_failure_mode: Defaults to generic systemic steroids and is unaware of recently approved targeted IgAN therapies, or misjudges the proteinuria threshold for escalating beyond supportive care.
items.why_high_value: The IgAN therapeutic landscape changed recently; models lag badly and give outdated escalation advice.
items.reference_basis: KDIGO 2025 IgAN/IgAV guidance and recent IgAN trial concepts (paraphrased).

items.prompt: An adult presents with palpable purpura, abdominal pain, and nephritis with hematuria and proteinuria consistent with IgA vasculitis. Outline how you assess renal involvement and the current approach to treating IgA vasculitis nephritis in adults.
items.ai_failure_mode: Treats adult IgA vasculitis nephritis as a purely self-limited pediatric illness, under-assessing or under-treating significant renal involvement, or extrapolating pediatric protocols inappropriately.
items.why_high_value: Adult IgA vasculitis nephritis is under-addressed and recently clarified in guidance; the assessment/treatment distinction is high-value.
items.reference_basis: KDIGO 2025 IgAV nephritis guidance concepts (paraphrased).

items.prompt: A patient with type 2 diabetes and CKD (eGFR 45, urine ACR 350 mg/g, potassium 4.6) is already on a maximally tolerated ACE inhibitor. Discuss adding an SGLT2 inhibitor and finerenone, including the order of introduction, eGFR/potassium thresholds, and monitoring.
items.ai_failure_mode: Omits one of the two kidney-protective pillars, introduces both simultaneously without a monitoring plan, or mismanages the potassium thresholds for finerenone initiation and continuation.
items.why_high_value: Combining and sequencing SGLT2i and finerenone on top of RAS blockade is current standard but recent and nuanced.
items.reference_basis: Finerenone and SGLT2i combination therapy concepts in diabetic kidney disease (paraphrased).

items.prompt: A non-diabetic patient with proteinuric CKD (eGFR 36) is started on an SGLT2 inhibitor for kidney protection. Counsel the patient on sick-day rules, what to expect with hydration and volume, and when to temporarily hold the medication.
items.ai_failure_mode: Restricts SGLT2 inhibitors to diabetic patients, omits sick-day guidance and volume considerations, or fails to explain temporary holds during acute illness.
items.why_high_value: Non-diabetic CKD indication plus sick-day counseling is current practice that models underuse or under-explain.
items.reference_basis: SGLT2 inhibitor use in non-diabetic CKD and sick-day guidance (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient with type 2 diabetes, obesity, and CKD (eGFR 40) is on an SGLT2 inhibitor and finerenone. They ask whether adding a GLP-1 receptor agonist offers additional kidney or cardiovascular benefit. Summarize the current evidence-based role and practical cautions.
items.ai_failure_mode: States GLP-1 receptor agonists have no kidney-relevant benefit (outdated), or fails to position the agent appropriately alongside the existing regimen and counsel on gastrointestinal and dose considerations.
items.why_high_value: Recent kidney-outcome evidence reshaped GLP-1 RA positioning; older-cutoff models give outdated framing.
items.reference_basis: Recent GLP-1 receptor agonist kidney-outcome evidence (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient with CKD and hypertension asks what blood pressure they should aim for. Explain the current systolic blood pressure target for many CKD patients, the critical caveat about how blood pressure must be measured to apply that target, and the main exceptions.
items.ai_failure_mode: Quotes an outdated higher blood pressure target, or cites the lower systolic target without the standardized/automated measurement caveat, leading to inappropriate treatment intensity.
items.why_high_value: The lower systolic target is tied to standardized measurement; models often drop the measurement caveat, which changes practice.
items.reference_basis: KDIGO blood pressure in CKD concepts including standardized measurement (paraphrased).

items.prompt: A patient with proteinuric diabetic CKD on an ACE inhibitor develops a potassium of 5.6 mEq/L, and a covering clinician stops the ACE inhibitor. Discuss the preferred contemporary approach to hyperkalemia that preserves kidney-protective therapy.
items.ai_failure_mode: Reflexively discontinues the renin-angiotensin system inhibitor for mild-to-moderate hyperkalemia rather than using dietary measures and potassium binders to maintain the kidney-protective agent.
items.why_high_value: Keeping RAS inhibition on board using novel binders, rather than stopping it, is current practice models frequently get wrong.
items.reference_basis: Guidance on maintaining RAS inhibition with potassium binders in hyperkalemia (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A non-dialysis CKD patient with anemia is being considered for an oral hypoxia-inducible factor prolyl-hydroxylase inhibitor instead of an injectable erythropoiesis-stimulating agent. Summarize where this newer class fits, the iron requirements, and the safety considerations.
items.ai_failure_mode: Is unaware of the HIF-PH inhibitor class or its place in therapy, neglects iron repletion needs, or overlooks the thrombotic and other safety signals informing its use.
items.why_high_value: HIF-PH inhibitors are a recent addition to renal anemia care that older-cutoff models do not position correctly.
items.reference_basis: Literature on HIF prolyl-hydroxylase inhibitors in CKD anemia (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: Review the medication list of a patient with type 2 diabetes and CKD (eGFR 42, urine ACR 500 mg/g): metformin, glipizide, lisinopril, atorvastatin. Identify the evidence-based kidney-protective therapies that are missing and justify each addition and the order you would introduce them.
items.ai_failure_mode: Fails to identify the missing pillars of modern diabetic kidney disease therapy (SGLT2 inhibitor, finerenone, and consideration of a GLP-1 receptor agonist), or cannot justify and sequence them safely.
items.why_high_value: Recognizing therapeutic gaps against current comprehensive DKD care is exactly the cutoff-lag, high-value judgment we target.
items.reference_basis: Comprehensive diabetic kidney disease therapy concepts (paraphrased).

items.prompt: A patient is two weeks out from a deceased-donor kidney transplant on tacrolimus, mycophenolate, and prednisone. Explain how you set and adjust the tacrolimus dose, what trough target range applies early after transplant, and how the target evolves over the first year.
items.ai_failure_mode: Uses a single fixed trough target regardless of time since transplant, ignores time-dependent target ranges, or adjusts dose without accounting for trough timing and interacting factors.
items.why_high_value: Time-dependent tacrolimus trough targets and titration are specialty-specific and easy to get wrong.
items.reference_basis: Transplant immunosuppression references on tacrolimus target troughs over time (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A kidney transplant recipient repeatedly has subtherapeutic tacrolimus troughs despite escalating doses and good adherence. Explain how pharmacogenomic variation in drug metabolism could account for this and how it would change your dosing strategy.
items.ai_failure_mode: Attributes persistently low troughs solely to nonadherence, missing rapid-metabolizer pharmacogenomics that require higher or more frequent dosing, or escalates blindly without checking interacting factors.
items.why_high_value: CYP3A5 expresser pharmacogenomics meaningfully change tacrolimus dosing and is frequently overlooked.
items.reference_basis: Pharmacogenomics references on CYP3A5 and tacrolimus dosing (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A stable kidney transplant recipient on tacrolimus, mycophenolate, and prednisone is planning pregnancy. Describe the immunosuppression changes you would make before conception and the rationale, plus what you would continue.
items.ai_failure_mode: Continues mycophenolate into a planned pregnancy despite its teratogenicity, fails to switch to a pregnancy-compatible agent such as azathioprine in advance, or stops all immunosuppression.
items.why_high_value: Pre-conception immunosuppression conversion is a safety-critical, concrete decision often mishandled.
items.reference_basis: Transplant and pregnancy immunosuppression safety references (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A kidney transplant recipient is switched to an mTOR inhibitor and afterward has poor surgical wound healing and new-onset proteinuria. Explain how the medication relates to these problems and how you would manage the situation.
items.ai_failure_mode: Does not connect mTOR inhibitor therapy to impaired wound healing and proteinuria, pursues unrelated workups, or stops the drug without an immunosuppression plan.
items.why_high_value: mTOR inhibitor adverse effects and their management are specialty-specific and commonly missed.
items.reference_basis: Transplant references on mTOR inhibitor adverse effects (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A high-risk kidney transplant recipient (donor positive, recipient negative for cytomegalovirus) is on valganciclovir prophylaxis and now has an eGFR of 35 and a falling white cell count. Discuss dose adjustment and how you balance prophylaxis against drug toxicity.
items.ai_failure_mode: Continues full-dose valganciclovir despite reduced eGFR (toxicity, leukopenia) or stops prophylaxis prematurely in a high-risk serostatus pair, or fails to adjust for the changing kidney function.
items.why_high_value: Renally dosed antiviral prophylaxis with a leukopenia tradeoff in a high-risk pair is specific and error-prone.
items.reference_basis: Transplant infectious disease prophylaxis references for cytomegalovirus (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A kidney transplant recipient develops new hyperglycemia meeting criteria for post-transplant diabetes a few months after transplant on tacrolimus-based immunosuppression. Discuss how the immunosuppression contributes and how you would weigh modifying it against rejection risk.
items.ai_failure_mode: Treats the hyperglycemia in isolation without considering calcineurin-inhibitor and steroid contributions, or proposes drastic immunosuppression changes that raise rejection risk without justification.
items.why_high_value: Post-transplant diabetes management requires balancing metabolic harm against rejection risk, a nuanced tradeoff.
items.reference_basis: References on post-transplant diabetes mellitus and immunosuppression (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A kidney transplant recipient has rising creatinine and new heavy proteinuria months after transplant, with donor-specific antibodies detected. Outline how you distinguish antibody-mediated rejection from recurrent glomerular disease and the principles of management for each.
items.ai_failure_mode: Assumes one diagnosis without biopsy and antibody correlation, conflates antibody-mediated rejection with recurrent primary disease, or applies the wrong treatment pathway.
items.why_high_value: Distinguishing antibody-mediated rejection from recurrent disease drives very different management and is high-stakes.
items.reference_basis: Transplant references on antibody-mediated rejection and recurrent glomerular disease (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: An adult with nephrotic-range proteinuria has biopsy-proven membranous nephropathy and a positive anti-PLA2R antibody. Discuss your approach to immunosuppression versus conservative therapy, how the antibody titer informs decisions, and which agent you would favor for immunosuppression.
items.ai_failure_mode: Treats all membranous patients with immediate immunosuppression regardless of risk, ignores anti-PLA2R titer trends in monitoring, or defaults to an outdated cytotoxic regimen over rituximab-based therapy.
items.why_high_value: PLA2R-guided, risk-based management of membranous nephropathy is current practice and nuanced.
items.reference_basis: KDIGO glomerular disease guidance on membranous nephropathy and PLA2R (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: An adult presents with abrupt-onset nephrotic syndrome and a biopsy showing minimal change disease. Outline initial therapy, the expected response, and how you would approach a relapse.
items.ai_failure_mode: Applies pediatric assumptions to adult minimal change disease, mis-doses or mis-times corticosteroids, or lacks a relapse/steroid-sparing strategy.
items.why_high_value: Adult minimal change management and relapse strategy differ from pediatric dogma and are commonly conflated.
items.reference_basis: KDIGO glomerular disease guidance on minimal change disease (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient with membranous nephropathy has heavy proteinuria and a serum albumin of 2.0 g/dL. Discuss whether prophylactic anticoagulation is indicated, how the albumin level and bleeding risk factor in, and which patients are highest risk for thrombosis.
items.ai_failure_mode: Either anticoagulates all nephrotic patients or none, ignoring the albumin threshold and the disease-specific (membranous) thrombosis risk and the bleeding-risk tradeoff.
items.why_high_value: Prophylactic anticoagulation in nephrotic syndrome is a specific risk-benefit judgment tied to albumin and histology.
items.reference_basis: Literature on thromboprophylaxis in nephrotic syndrome (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient has biopsy-proven focal segmental glomerulosclerosis. Explain how you would distinguish a primary (immune-mediated) form from a secondary/adaptive form, and why that distinction completely changes whether you use immunosuppression.
items.ai_failure_mode: Treats all FSGS with immunosuppression without separating primary from secondary forms, missing that adaptive/secondary FSGS is managed with RAS blockade and addressing the driver rather than immunosuppression.
items.why_high_value: The primary-versus-secondary FSGS distinction is decisive for therapy and frequently missed.
items.reference_basis: Glomerular disease references on primary versus secondary FSGS (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient with long-standing diabetes has worsening proteinuria but also has hematuria, an absence of retinopathy, and a rapid rise in creatinine. Discuss when diabetic nephropathy can be assumed clinically versus when a kidney biopsy is warranted to exclude another glomerular disease.
items.ai_failure_mode: Attributes all kidney disease in a diabetic patient to diabetic nephropathy and misses atypical features (no retinopathy, active sediment, rapid decline) that should prompt a biopsy.
items.why_high_value: Recognizing atypical features that warrant biopsy in a diabetic patient is a high-value clinical judgment.
items.reference_basis: References on biopsy indications in diabetic patients with kidney disease (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient presents with rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis, hemoptysis, and a high anti-glomerular basement membrane antibody titer, with a creatinine of 6 mg/dL and oliguria. Outline induction therapy including the role of plasma exchange and how dialysis dependence at presentation affects renal prognosis and decisions.
items.ai_failure_mode: Omits or delays urgent plasma exchange in anti-GBM disease, mismanages combined immunosuppression, or fails to factor dialysis-dependence and biopsy findings into the renal-recovery prognosis.
items.why_high_value: Anti-GBM disease is a time-critical emergency where plasma exchange timing and prognosis judgments are decisive.
items.reference_basis: References on anti-GBM disease management and plasma exchange (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A young adult has a membranoproliferative pattern on biopsy with dominant C3 staining and low serum C3. Explain how you would work up the complement pathway abnormality and the principles guiding therapy for C3 glomerulopathy.
items.ai_failure_mode: Treats a C3-dominant membranoproliferative pattern as ordinary immune-complex disease, fails to evaluate the alternative complement pathway, or proposes therapy without the complement framework.
items.why_high_value: C3 glomerulopathy requires a complement-pathway workup and specialized management that models rarely capture.
items.reference_basis: References on C3 glomerulopathy and complement evaluation (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient with class IV lupus nephritis has completed induction and achieved a partial response. Discuss maintenance immunosuppression, the role of hydroxychloroquine, the place of newer agents such as a calcineurin inhibitor add-on, and monitoring for relapse.
items.ai_failure_mode: Neglects maintenance therapy planning or hydroxychloroquine, is unaware of newer add-on agents (e.g., voclosporin/belimumab), or lacks a relapse-monitoring strategy.
items.why_high_value: Lupus nephritis maintenance and the modern add-on landscape are recent and frequently under-addressed.
items.reference_basis: KDIGO and rheumatology lupus nephritis maintenance guidance (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient with chronic hepatitis C presents with palpable purpura, low complement, a positive cryoglobulin, and nephritis with proteinuria and hematuria. Explain how you would treat the underlying driver and when immunosuppression or plasma exchange is added for severe disease.
items.ai_failure_mode: Jumps to immunosuppression without prioritizing antiviral therapy for the hepatitis C driver, or fails to reserve plasma exchange and immunosuppression for severe organ-threatening disease.
items.why_high_value: Sequencing antiviral therapy versus immunosuppression in cryoglobulinemic GN is a nuanced, high-value decision.
items.reference_basis: References on hepatitis C-associated cryoglobulinemic glomerulonephritis (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: An ICU patient with sepsis and CKD stage 3 urgently needs a contrast-enhanced CT to find a source. The team wants to delay imaging and give prophylactic N-acetylcysteine and a bicarbonate drip first. Critically evaluate this plan using current evidence on contrast-associated AKI.
items.ai_failure_mode: Endorses delaying essential imaging and giving N-acetylcysteine or routine bicarbonate as clearly protective, overstating contrast nephrotoxicity rather than emphasizing isotonic fluids and not withholding needed imaging.
items.why_high_value: Contrast-AKI evidence shifted substantially; models give outdated, harm-causing prophylaxis and imaging-avoidance advice.
items.reference_basis: Contemporary contrast-associated AKI evidence on risk and prophylaxis (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A critically ill patient with AKI is on continuous renal replacement therapy. Explain how you set the effluent dose, what target is appropriate, and how being on CRRT changes antibiotic dosing for a patient with severe sepsis.
items.ai_failure_mode: Misstates the effluent dose target, under-doses antibiotics during high-clearance CRRT (risking treatment failure), or ignores CRRT-specific drug clearance entirely.
items.why_high_value: CRRT dose targets and CRRT antibiotic dosing are quantitative, high-stakes, and frequently wrong without nephrology/ID input.
items.reference_basis: Critical-care nephrology references on CRRT dose and drug dosing (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient on a loop diuretic develops AKI, and a fractional excretion of sodium comes back at 0.5%. The covering team concludes the AKI is prerenal. Critique this interpretation and explain a more reliable approach to distinguishing prerenal AKI from acute tubular necrosis in this setting.
items.ai_failure_mode: Accepts a low FeNa as proof of prerenal AKI despite diuretic use confounding it, rather than using fractional excretion of urea and the broader clinical picture.
items.why_high_value: FeNa pitfalls (diuretics, CKD) are a classic reasoning trap with a specific correction (FeUrea, context).
items.reference_basis: References on FeNa and FeUrea interpretation in AKI (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient with acute decompensated heart failure has worsening kidney function and remains congested despite escalating intravenous loop diuretics. Discuss diuretic resistance, the rationale for sequential nephron blockade, and how you interpret a rising creatinine during effective decongestion.
items.ai_failure_mode: Stops diuresis at the first creatinine bump despite ongoing congestion, fails to escalate with sequential nephron blockade, or misreads decongestion-related creatinine changes as harmful AKI.
items.why_high_value: Cardiorenal diuretic resistance and the meaning of creatinine changes during decongestion are nuanced judgment calls.
items.reference_basis: Cardiorenal syndrome and diuretic resistance references (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient with septic shock and early AKI has received initial fluids but remains hypotensive, and the team plans large additional crystalloid boluses. Discuss the balance between perfusion and fluid overload in sepsis-associated AKI and your choice of resuscitation fluid.
items.ai_failure_mode: Recommends unchecked large-volume fluids without weighing fluid overload harm and the role of vasopressors, or defaults to high-chloride saline rather than balanced crystalloids.
items.why_high_value: Fluid strategy and fluid choice in sepsis-associated AKI is an evolving, judgment-heavy area models oversimplify.
items.reference_basis: Critical-care references on fluid strategy and balanced crystalloids in sepsis-associated AKI (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A critically ill patient with stage 3 AKI has a potassium of 5.3 mEq/L, a pH of 7.30, and no other absolute dialysis indication. The team wants to start renal replacement therapy immediately to get ahead of it. Discuss accelerated versus standard timing of initiation using current evidence.
items.ai_failure_mode: Asserts that earlier/accelerated RRT improves survival in the absence of emergent indications, contrary to recent trial evidence favoring a watchful, indication-driven approach.
items.why_high_value: RRT initiation timing is a recently clarified, counterintuitive area where models overstate the benefit of starting early.
items.reference_basis: Trial evidence on accelerated versus standard RRT initiation timing in AKI (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A postoperative patient with massive fluid resuscitation, a tense distended abdomen, rising airway pressures, and oliguric AKI is not responding to fluids. Explain the diagnosis you must consider, how it causes AKI, and how confirming it would change management.
items.ai_failure_mode: Treats the oliguria as simple hypovolemia and gives more fluid, missing intra-abdominal hypertension/abdominal compartment syndrome and the need to measure intra-abdominal pressure and decompress.
items.why_high_value: Abdominal compartment syndrome as a reversible AKI cause is easily missed, and recognizing it reverses the fluid strategy.
items.reference_basis: Critical-care references on intra-abdominal hypertension and AKI (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A 9-year-old with CKD stage 3 and hypertension needs an antihypertensive and has a urinary tract infection requiring an antibiotic. Explain how drug dosing and eGFR estimation differ in children compared with adults, including which estimating equation applies and how weight-based dosing interacts with renal adjustment.
items.ai_failure_mode: Applies adult eGFR equations and adult fixed doses to a child, ignoring pediatric-specific estimating equations and weight-based dosing, risking under- or over-dosing.
items.why_high_value: Pediatric eGFR estimation and weight-based renal dosing are distinct from adult practice and frequently mishandled.
items.reference_basis: Pediatric nephrology references on eGFR estimation and weight-based renal dosing (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient on maintenance hemodialysis with advancing dementia, recurrent hospitalizations, and declining function is becoming increasingly distressed by dialysis. The family asks about stopping. Frame a goals-of-care discussion about dialysis withdrawal, including prognosis after withdrawal and the palliative supports you would arrange.
items.ai_failure_mode: Treats dialysis as obligatory and avoids discussing withdrawal as a legitimate option, omits the expected prognosis after stopping, or fails to arrange palliative/hospice support.
items.why_high_value: Dialysis withdrawal is an ethically and clinically nuanced shared decision that models tend to avoid or mishandle.
items.reference_basis: Shared decision-making and conservative/palliative kidney care references (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: An 82-year-old with low muscle mass and a normal-appearing serum creatinine is about to start a renally-cleared medication with a narrow therapeutic window. Explain why creatinine-based eGFR may mislead here and how cystatin C-based estimation would change your dosing decision.
items.ai_failure_mode: Trusts a normal creatinine-based eGFR in a low-muscle-mass elderly patient and overestimates kidney function, leading to overdosing, rather than using cystatin C to refine the estimate.
items.why_high_value: Creatinine overestimates GFR in low muscle mass; cystatin C correction is a high-value, frequently overlooked dosing safeguard.
items.reference_basis: References on cystatin C-based GFR estimation in low muscle mass (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient with severe obesity (body mass index 48) needs a renally-cleared medication dosed by kidney function. Explain the pitfalls of using a body-surface-area-indexed eGFR for drug dosing in this patient and how you would estimate kidney function for dosing decisions.
items.ai_failure_mode: Uses the indexed (mL/min/1.73m2) eGFR directly for drug dosing in a severely obese patient without de-indexing to an absolute clearance, leading to systematic dosing error.
items.why_high_value: The indexed-versus-absolute GFR distinction for drug dosing in obesity is technical and commonly gotten wrong.
items.reference_basis: References on indexed versus absolute GFR for drug dosing in obesity (paraphrased concepts).

items.prompt: A patient with decompensated cirrhosis is admitted with a urinary infection and early kidney dysfunction, and the team is choosing antibiotics, analgesia, and diuretic management. Discuss how you avoid precipitating further kidney injury and hepatorenal physiology in this setting, including drugs to avoid and volume strategy.
items.ai_failure_mode: Uses nephrotoxic drugs (NSAIDs, aminoglycosides) or aggressive diuresis that precipitates hepatorenal-type injury, or fails to recognize the heightened susceptibility to AKI in cirrhosis.
items.why_high_value: Protecting kidney function in cirrhosis requires specific avoidances and volume judgment that span hepatology and nephrology.
items.reference_basis: References on AKI prevention and hepatorenal physiology in cirrhosis (paraphrased concepts).
